3: The energy scenario has been facing a crisis due to the indis- criminate extraction and abundant consumption of fossil fuels, which has led to fossil fuel depletion and environmental degrada- tion. This situation has stimulated the search for alternative fuels characterized as non-fossil, renewable, and non-polluting (Agarwal, 2007). Lignocellulosic biomass is known as a source of clean energy, and low-carbon biofuels are an important element in bioenergy development (Borrion et al., 2012).

Over the past 20 years, bioethanol has been one of the leading candidates to replace a fraction of the liquid fuels produced from oil and has become prominent in the conversion of biomass resources for the production of biofuels (Megawati et al., 2011). Bioethanol

can be used in engines specially designed for its use. However, it can also be used in mixtures of up to 10% with gasoline without engine modifications or in a greater proportion (85%) in flexi-fuel vehicles (Hamelinck et al., 2005; Kumar and Murthy, 2011). The main sources worldwide for ethanol production are materials rich in sugar and starch. The USA and Brazil produce ethanol from corn and sugar cane, respectively, while in Europe and in China, cereals are used as raw materials (Morales et al., 2015). With 89% of the total production, the USA and Brazil are the main producers of bioethanol in the world (Limayem and Ricke, 2012). Although it has been a promising substitute for gasoline, this biofuel production only replaces a minimal portion of the worldwide consumption of fossils fuels.

However, land use issues and threats to food security have made biofuel production controversial, mainly due to the uncertainties of the environmental benefits and the social disadvantages (Paschalidou et al., 2016).

Alternatively, using waste as raw material may be a more promising option for biofuel production (Nasterlack et al., 2014) because the first-generation biofuels require the growth of food. To be sustainable, value chains of biomass to bioenergy products require the successful deployment of innovative biotechnologies.

Residual biomasses, such as agro-industrial byproducts and residues and animal manures, represent the spatially diffused sources of biofuel substrates. In the agro-industry there is a wide variety of organic feedstock, such as lignocellulosic materials, vegetable oils, animal fats, protein-rich waste, animal slurries and manure (Schievano et al., 2009).

Lignocellulosic biomass has received special attention in biofuel production because of concerns over high fuel prices, energy sup- plies, global climate change, and diversification for rural economic development (González-García et al., 2013). Moreover, according to González-García et al. (2012) second-generation biofuels derived from lignocellulosic biomass are environmentally attractive in terms of biomass exploitation, such as wood and agricultural waste. Cost-effective sources of energy are important because production technology is still under development.

Recently, several studies assessed the environmental perfor- mance of the production and use of cellulosic bioethanol (e.g., eucalyptus (González-García et al., 2012), black locust (González- García et al., 2011), wheat (Biswas et al., 2008), switchgrass (Morales 2015), straw (Kravanja et al., 2012) and grass straws (Kumar and Murthy, 2012)). The main objective was to compare the environmental impact of bioethanol produced from lignocellulose and by the most diverse production methods. The most commonly used approach is the life cycle assessment (LCA), which is a methodological tool used to quantitatively analyze the life cycle of a product or an activity within a generic frame provided by SO:14040 (2006) and :14044 (2006).

Many bioethanol production paths have already been evaluated using LCA tools as presented in Table 1. In these studies there is a focus on the determination of global warming potential, which is established from the amount of CO2-eq of all stages of production. The results demonstrate the concern with the type of biomass to be transformed, with the process and with the possibility of this liquid fuel being responsible for impact reduction attributed to the use of fossil fuels. However, the use of residues as feedstock for biofuel is still excipient, and there are no studies that evaluate the impacts of using animal residues with high fiber content convertible to sugars and ethanol. LCA studies have also been conducted from cellulosic material in general, which is acceptable, because the transformation method- ology is sufficiently robust. Without large-scale production, the potential environmental performance of many proposed procedures remains unclear. Therefore, the LCA tool allows the identification of potential im- pacts in an initial phase of the design process and provides the opportunity for decision-making to improve the process for sus- tainability before it is scaled up or fully implemented. As in Table 1, there is process optimization and interest in demonstrating that it is environmentally friendly. In this way, many efforts have been made in the last decades to develop a commercial process for producing second-generation bioethanol using lignocellulosic biomass (Cardona et al., 2010), which combines both economic and environmental factors. One example is the large pollutant load that is not properly managed from the waste generated by feedlot cattle. If these resi- dues reach water sources, they will increase the chemical oxygen demand, reduce the oxygen content of the water and, finally, cause the death of fish and other organisms. Furthermore, the presence of nutrients such as N, P and K stimulates the growth of aquatic plants, which may cause water eutrophication. Cattle manure (CM)

contains undigested lignocellulosic material, the composition of which depends on the diet and can be easily separated in confined systems. Overall, cattle convert only 30%-40% of the ingested food in production (Kozen and Alvarenga, 2005).

Generally, the final destination of manure is lagooning, direct application to land, composting, anaerobic digestion or combus- tion, all of which have environmental impacts related to CH4, N20 and NH3 emissions. In combustion the only problematic emission is N20 (Sagastume Gutiérrez et al., 2016). However, there is already an established market for biogas production by anaerobic digestion that is successful for energy production as more energy efficient technologies come into use (Fuchsz and Kohlheb, 2015). Thus, manure is a little-explored reservoir of biomass for energy pro- duction, such as ethanol (Liao et al., 2014).

Because production cost is extremely sensitive to biofuel feed- stock costs, waste can be an alternative to feedstock with high starch or sugar content; however, challenges such as biomass transport and handling and difficulties within the process must be considered (Bhutto et al., 2015). In addition to developing a tech- nological framework, it is important to consider the emissions and inputs required to achieve the maximum process efficiency.

Based on aspects of the production of bioethanol from ligno- cellulosic biomass, the main objective of this study was to apply the LCA to the production of cellulosic bioethanol from CM. For this purpose, the process was examined at the laboratory scale using parameters that have already been optimized by (Vancov et al., 2015). The data were organized according to the process estab- lished in Vancov et al.'s study with Australian cattle manure and conducted on the laboratory scale with cattle manure from the south of Brazil, and the Evaluation of the Life Cycle was performed using Simapro software 7.3.2.

Following the research mentioned above, the present study was designed to enhance the insight into environmental performance of bioethanol from CM by applying LCA as a support for decision making in the context of green chemistry and eco-innovation strategies.

10: LCA is a compilation and evaluation of the inputs, outputs and the potential environmental impacts of a process or product system throughout its life cycle. A product or process causes impacts on its ecosystem, during production, distribution, consumption, disposal and recycling (ISO-14040, 2006). LCA includes the following steps: Goal and scope definition, Inventory analysis, Impact assessment and Interpretation, all of which were followed in this work.

The life cycle assessment method was used to evaluate the environmental impacts of all stages of CM bioethanol production. The research was initially performed on a laboratory scale from August 2013 to December 2014. The equipment selected for the pilot configuration for CM bioethanol production was equipment that is commonly used for several biomass preparations and transformations and can be found in the domestic market. The equipment had the characteristics, size, power consumption and other necessary attributes for assessment. The procedure proposed for the production of bioethanol was Separated Hydrolysis and Fermentation (SHF) using previously optimized conditions. The Life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) was carried out before the instal- lation of equipment. This tool was used for support decisions about the stages and care of lignocellulosic bioethanol production. The procedures to assess the environmental impacts of CM bioethanol production have been further detailed.

The cattle manure was considered zero-burden. Some biofuels from residues falling under the zero-burden assumption might fulfill the sustainability criteria (Boldrin and Astrup, 2015). of CM (Table 2) bioethanol production and interpret the results to determine the best strategy for reducing the environmental impact of bioethanol production in the future in order to identify potential environmental consequences associated with the conversion of residual lignocellulosic material in the manure into bioethanol. Therefore, the paper aims to clarify the potential envi- ronmental benefits of applying this technology in Brazil and others countries.

manure. This is consistent with the available primary data.

The cattle manure was considered zero-burden. Some biofuels from residues falling under the zero-burden assumption might fulfill the sustainability criteria (Boldrin and Astrup, 2015). The zero-burden assumption concept is a LCA system simplification and allows the focus to be on the ethanol production process.

In addition, biofuel production stages did not include biomass feedstock production because this study aims to take advantage of the manure as feedstock. In the production of cattle, the manure is always a residue with high environmental impact with or without treatment, as previously studied (Budde et al., 2016; Huerta et al., 2016; Lupo et al., 2013; Mezzullo et al., 2013). All the impacts considered in the manure production could give a different inter- pretation to the process. These impacts are intrinsic to this biomass. However, its use for the production of biofuel is what is being evaluated in our study.

Thus, ethanol production can be a way to give the manure an industrial applicability and may be a source to generate income for the farmer. After production, the distribution follows the same paths of ethanol produced by other raw materials, which have already been studied (Wang et al., 2014).

The functional unit corresponds to 871.6 MJ of ethanol according to INEN (2016) where 1 L of ethanol corresponds to an energy content of 20.09 MJ. This production corresponds to 25 animals that generate 40 kg day- animal-¹ of manure when confined or 66 animals that generate 15 kg day- animal- of manure when semi- confined (Freitas, 2008). In addition, it is emphasized that this functional unit was defined due to the pilot plant equipment ca- pacity proposed for the research in the Regional Science and Technology Park, since the plant can be used for various lignocel- lulosic materials. The differential is the drying step of the manure, which is not necessary for other lignocellulosic matrices.
The equipment should be installed next to the manure collec- tion site to avoid having to transport the materials. This unit for bioethanol production was proposed to be installed in a rural area.

19: <header>2.2. Life cycle inventory </header>

The inventory data for CM bioethanol were calculated and analyzed from primary and secondary sources. Raw materials in the production steps were collected as primary data from laboratory- scale experiments and in previous research work (Vancov et al.,

2015), and energy consumption values were from the equipment manufacturer's information. In relation to energy consumption, Brazil's electricity mix contains fossil (12.46%), hydropower (80.29%), nuclear (2.88%) and renewables (4.32%) (Itten et al., 2012).

Secondary information for items such as enzyme production was found in the literature and the Ecoinvent database version 2.1 was used for items such as the production of chemicals. The in- ventory data were divided into six parts: drying, milling, pre- treatment with acid, enzyme treatment, fermentation and distil- lation. The data related to emissions and consumption of raw ma- terial were converted to a scale of 1000 kg of CM collected on a wet- basis, corresponding to 193 kg of dry biomass.
Each step is described below, and all inventory data are pre- sented in Table 3 based on an initial wet manure mass of 1000 kg.

The biomass drying was performed in a rotatory dryer (SRL500 Lippel brand) with a drying capacity of 1000 kg h-1. The engine for moving the biomass in the dryer was rated as 3728 kW, the engine for delivering the air inside the dryer as 746 kW and the engine for removing the condensed water as 746 kW. The resulting energy consumption for drying the biomass was 552,2 kWh, with the drying time for 1000 kg of biomass being approximately 48 min. At this stage, the vaporization energy of the water contained in manure was calculated to be 44 kJ mol-¹

Therefore, 1,973,115 k] would be required for the evaporation of 807 kg of water contained in 1000 kg of manure collected. During the biomass milling the only input was energy (1.3 kWh) for a hammermill (Carlos Becker brand MMB-270/20) with 2400 kg h -Superscript(1) of milling capacity.
The acid pretreatment required an autoclave (Phoenix brand AHVC-1000), a 2.5% solution of H2SO4 for 1 h at 121 °C with 10% load. The energy consumption of this step was 84 kWh.

After the acid hydrolysis, phase separation (hydrolysate and solid phase) and autoclave washing using 50 L of H2O and 1 L of 15% NaClO were required. For solid phase separation a decanter (Limana Poliservices brand) with 2000-L capacity and a 20 mesh grid was proposed.

The solid phase was forwarded to the enzymatic hydrolysis and the liquid phase was considered to be waste that could be for- warded to the production of biopolymers (in another process) because this phase is rich in pentoses and the yeast Saccharomyces cerevisae does not use these sugars in fermentation (Wiloso et al., 2012).

The solid phase was washed with water until the pH 5.0. The final solid phase from acid pretreatment was quantified at the laboratory scale, representing 44.71%. The equipment was cleaned with approximately 50 L of 3% sodium hypochlorite.
For the solid phase saccharification the enzymatic hydrolysis was conducted at a 5% load in a pH 5.0 buffer in a bioreactor (WFA brand).

The Cellic® CTec 2 enzyme (Novozymes) recommended for the hydrolysis of polysaccharides rich in cellulose was used in the lab scale saccharification. As this enzyme is not in the libraries that are available in SimaPro V. 7.3.2, the impact values used were obtained for the production process of another enzyme (Spirizymes® Plus FG) from Novozymes (Nielsen et al., 2007).

Thus, considering that the enzymatic hydrolysis occurred at 50 °C for 72 h, and the predicted biomass after pre-treatment was 86.29 kg, 466 kWh were required in the saccharification. After that, the liquid phase (hydrolysate) was forwarded for fermentation while the separated solid phase (14.62%) could be used for soil fertilization. At the end of the saccharification, the same volume of sodium hypochlorite previously calculated for the pretreatment was required to clean the equipment.
In the fermentation, the hydrolysate was sterilized for 30 min and fermentation was conducted in bioreactor for 8 h, and

proportional energy consumption was 73 kWh. The total hydroly- sate (1547.72 kg) includes the nutrients and yeast for fermentation.

Subsequently, the CO2 emissions that occurred during fermen- tation were quantified based on the quantity of bioethanol deter- mined experimentally. The amount of bioethanol produced in 8 h of fermentation was 17.74 g of bioethanol/100 g of dried biomass. Thus, for 193 kg of dried biomass the CO2 emission from the fermentation would be 32.75 kg.

Distillation was carried out in equipment (Limana Services) that requires 180 kWh of energy for 4 h. The condensation/cooling water as well as the steam water used to heat the bioreactor were not considered as entries in the inventory, as both were recircu- lated. The solid waste can be applied in the soil for fertilization along with manure to reduce use of mineral fertilizers for cash crops (Marton et al., 2016).

In the total process seven steps were studied, and it was possible to obtain 33.6 kg of bioethanol under the selected conditions, but it is expected that the efficiency can be improved after pilot plant installation.

Therefore, 1,973,115 k] would be required for the evaporation of 807 kg of water contained in 1000 kg of manure collected. During the biomass milling the only input was energy (1.3 kWh) for a hammermill (Carlos Becker brand MMB-270/20) with 2400 kg h -Superscript(1) of milling capacity.
The acid pretreatment required an autoclave (Phoenix brand AHVC-1000), a 2.5% solution of H2SO4 for 1 h at 121 °C with 10% load. The energy consumption of this step was 84 kWh.

After the acid hydrolysis, phase separation (hydrolysate and solid phase) and autoclave washing using 50 L of H2O and 1 L of 15% NaClO were required. For solid phase separation a decanter (Limana Poliservices brand) with 2000-L capacity and a 20 mesh grid was proposed.

26: After inventory of the inputs and outputs of each step for raw materials, energy, gas, and solid and liquid emissions, the life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) was obtained using SimaPro V. 7.3.2 (LCA software).

The scenarios for each individual step and of the entire process were examined. The environmental midpoint and endpoint in- dicators were studied to determine the potential environmental impacts. These indicators were chosen because the midpoint pre- sents the cause-effect chain between the stressors and the end- points and, the endpoints allow aggregation for the level of damage (Bare and Gloria, 2008).

A recognized characterization method, namely ReCiPe V1.06, available in the SimaPro software, was used to consider the following categories: climate change (CC), ozone depletion (OD), terrestrial acidification (TA), freshwater eutrophication (FE), marine eutrophication (ME), human toxicity (HT), photochemical oxidant formation (POF), particulate matter formation (PMF), terrestrial ecotoxicity (TET), freshwater ecotoxicity (FET), marine ecotoxicity (MET), ionizing radiation (IR), agricultural land occupation (ALO), urban land occupation (ULO), natural land transformation (NLT), water depletion (WD), mineral resource depletion (MRD) and fossil

Furthermore, for the statistical assessment, the Monte Carlo simulation was performed with SimaPro® V. 7.3.2 (version 7.3.2), which allows for uncertainty propagation over all of the parameters with 10,000 independent simulations. The data summarized in the inventory phase were interpreted based on categories of the ReCiPe characterization method. In the LCIA of the CM bioethanol pro- duction, the importance of each step was evaluated for the envi- ronmental impacts in the process.

Furthermore, one-way sensitivity analysis is determined by the amount an individual input parameter value needs to change, with all other parameters held constant, in order for output parameter values to change by a certain percentage (Björklund, 2002). This assessment was conducted for energy type in the process and for washing water only in separation phases.

28: Bioethanol production from CM, a residue that requires treat- ment for disposal, receives high impact compensation. There is an important environmental gain from ethanol production when it uses a raw material that is a residue from another process.

In the case of CM, the impact of these residues was reduced because the process eliminates the problem of adequate disposal of the manure, to which are assigned impacts on Global Warming Potential, Aquatic and Terrestrial Eutrophication potential. Ac- cording to Cherubini et al. (2015) the manure management system contributed as high as 59.7% and 78.6% of the total impact for freshwater eutrophication and terrestrial ecotoxicity, respectively. This arises because manure management can generate emissions such as carbon dioxide, ammonia, phosphorus and nitrates, which contribute to climate change, terrestrial acidification, freshwater eutrophication and marine eutrophication.

According to Battini et al. (2014), when manure was stored in an open tank and then used as fertilizer, up to 82% of the total impact was due to ammonia emissions. Similarly, this author analyzed the impacts on the employment of manure to produce biogas and determined that NOx emissions increase with the combustion of biogas and SO2 emissions decrease because of the fossil electricity substituted.
Thus, the use of manure for the bioethanol production may
reduce the impacts due to other common fates of this residue. The results of assessment of the impacts of the life cycle of bioethanol production from cattle manure are shown in Table 4.

Negative values of LCIA presented in the table are compensa- tions of the manure disposal and treatment impacts. Fig. 2 shows that the major environmental gains using CM as a bioethanol raw material occurred in the drying step, in which CM is an input. The impacts in several categories were negative. In the categories of marine eutrophication, terrestrial acidification, particulate matter formation, ozone depletion and climate change the impacts were 40%-100% negative and demonstrate the benefits of the use of CM as a raw material in the process. The negative values indicate sav- ings in the use of manure and the achievement of related envi- ronmental benefits, mainly the reduction of the release of compounds responsible for ozone depletion, human toxicity and water eutrophication. Ripa et al. (2017) also found that there was compensation in the AICV results. They interpreted that the pro- duction of organic compost from manure to substitute equivalent amounts of inorganic fertilizers saved impacts related to manure disposal, besides other gains from the use of natural products as more stabilized forms of carbon.

Cattle manure to bioethanol conversion avoids problems with pathogens present in animal manure that can cause a number of humans disease and environmental contamination by nutrients (eg, N and P) in the soil (Lorenz and Lal, 2016).

Despite the impacts that were avoided by the use of CM as the raw material, the drying was the step with the most environmental impact, due to the energy consumption and the emissions from CM during the drying.

In the method using harmonized category indicators at the midpoint level, all impact categories in the process were repre- sented. The most contributing processes to each relevant impact category were enzymatic hydrolysis and separation phases. In the enzymatic hydrolysis step, presented in Fig. 3, there were contri- butions in all categories due to energy, enzyme, disposal of the bio- waste in agriculture after hydrolysis, and use of buffer for pH control in the process. Gerbrandt et al. (2016) noted that the pro- portion of the enzyme used has a direct effect on greenhouse gas

emissions due to the carbon intensity of the enzyme production. Electricity was not used in the separation of the solid phase after the pretreatment because filtration by gravity was used. However, in this step of the process improvement will be possible by the use of centrifugation. Electricity added impacts related mainly to climate change, human toxicity, freshwater and marine ecotoxicity. Climate change and human toxicity are the main contributors to the total of the potential human health impacts (HHI). The HHI corrupts the quality of life of each individual affected both in the short and in the long term and leads to economic impacts for the whole society, which must defray the costs of health treatment (Treyer et al., 2014).

ecotoxicity. The amount of carbon dioxide produced in the fermentation reaction is low compared to all the carbon dioxide calculated for the process. Ebner et al. (2014) investigated the im- pacts of the waste-to-ethanol process as an alternative biofuel pathway in terms of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and treated the carbon dioxide of the fermentation as biogenic and did not included in the GHG emission inventories. We included this gas in the inventory and the LCIA with normalized data that were lower than the chemical compound and electricity inputs.

There are no studies based on manure to ethanol that can be directly used as a benchmark for our findings. However, we find that these results are generally in line with those reported by other lignocellulosic bioethanol studies.

Moreover, in the climate change category there is an environ- mental impact ratio of 1.51 kg CO2 eq to 43.4 L of bioethanol pro- duced, corresponding to 0.0017 kg CO2 eq MJ-1, where 1 L of bioethanol corresponds to an energy content of 20.09 MJ (INEN, 2016). This value is less than the value obtained by Mayer et al. (2015), who determined 0.128 kg CO2 eq MJ-1 in a small-scale ethanol production from sugar cane where 54% of the CO2 eq emissions were at the industrial stage (0.06912 kg CO2 eq MJ-1). According to Rocha et al. (2014), at the industrial scale it is possible to achieve 0.00626 kg CO2 eq MJ or less, involving agricultural sugar cane production in the scope of the research.

According to Costa Junior et al. (2015) the total measured and estimated GHG emissions from housing to field application comprised of 15.0 + 7.4 kg CO2-eq animal which 80% corre- sponded to the field application of manure, this is approximately 12 kg CO2-eq animal-¹ For bioethanol production in the functional unit proposal in our study we identified a generate of 1.51 kg CO2- eq, approximately 25 feedlot cattle (0.06 kg CO2-eq animal-¹. The GHG estimated mitigation is 95% the same that Costa Junior et al. (2015) found for electricity cogeneration and N-fertilizer produc- tion from feedlot cattle manure.
Besides that, as presented previously in Table 1 from several assessment and production methods, the GWP from many ligno- cellulosics feedstooks is lower than from fossil fuel.

These analyses demonstrate that bioethanol derived from CM can be interesting for agro-industrial diversification. CM is a dangerous residue for the environment and its main impacts can be minimized when used in large-scale ethanol production.
31: Bioethanol production from CM, a residue that requires treat- ment for disposal, receives high impact compensation. There is an important environmental gain from ethanol production when it uses a raw material that is a residue from another process.

In the case of CM, the impact of these residues was reduced because the process eliminates the problem of adequate disposal of the manure, to which are assigned impacts on Global Warming Potential, Aquatic and Terrestrial Eutrophication potential. Ac- cording to Cherubini et al. (2015) the manure management system contributed as high as 59.7% and 78.6% of the total impact for freshwater eutrophication and terrestrial ecotoxicity, respectively. This arises because manure management can generate emissions such as carbon dioxide, ammonia, phosphorus and nitrates, which contribute to climate change, terrestrial acidification, freshwater eutrophication and marine eutrophication.

According to Battini et al. (2014), when manure was stored in an open tank and then used as fertilizer, up to 82% of the total impact was due to ammonia emissions. Similarly, this author analyzed the impacts on the employment of manure to produce biogas and determined that NOx emissions increase with the combustion of biogas and SO2 emissions decrease because of the fossil electricity substituted.
Thus, the use of manure for the bioethanol production may
reduce the impacts due to other common fates of this residue. The results of assessment of the impacts of the life cycle of bioethanol production from cattle manure are shown in Table 4.

Negative values of LCIA presented in the table are compensa- tions of the manure disposal and treatment impacts. Fig. 2 shows that the major environmental gains using CM as a bioethanol raw material occurred in the drying step, in which CM is an input. The impacts in several categories were negative. In the categories of marine eutrophication, terrestrial acidification, particulate matter formation, ozone depletion and climate change the impacts were 40%-100% negative and demonstrate the benefits of the use of CM as a raw material in the process. The negative values indicate sav- ings in the use of manure and the achievement of related envi- ronmental benefits, mainly the reduction of the release of compounds responsible for ozone depletion, human toxicity and water eutrophication. Ripa et al. (2017) also found that there was compensation in the AICV results. They interpreted that the pro- duction of organic compost from manure to substitute equivalent amounts of inorganic fertilizers saved impacts related to manure disposal, besides other gains from the use of natural products as more stabilized forms of carbon.

Cattle manure to bioethanol conversion avoids problems with pathogens present in animal manure that can cause a number of humans disease and environmental contamination by nutrients (eg, N and P) in the soil (Lorenz and Lal, 2016).

Despite the impacts that were avoided by the use of CM as the raw material, the drying was the step with the most environmental impact, due to the energy consumption and the emissions from CM during the drying.

In the method using harmonized category indicators at the midpoint level, all impact categories in the process were repre- sented. The most contributing processes to each relevant impact category were enzymatic hydrolysis and separation phases. In the enzymatic hydrolysis step, presented in Fig. 3, there were contri- butions in all categories due to energy, enzyme, disposal of the bio- waste in agriculture after hydrolysis, and use of buffer for pH control in the process. Gerbrandt et al. (2016) noted that the pro- portion of the enzyme used has a direct effect on greenhouse gas
Fig. 2. The characterization (%) of the life cycle impact assessment from CM bioethanol production by ReCiPe Midpoint (H) V1.06.

emissions due to the carbon intensity of the enzyme production. Electricity was not used in the separation of the solid phase after the pretreatment because filtration by gravity was used. However, in this step of the process improvement will be possible by the use of centrifugation. Electricity added impacts related mainly to climate change, human toxicity, freshwater and marine ecotoxicity. Climate change and human toxicity are the main contributors to the total of the potential human health impacts (HHI). The HHI corrupts the quality of life of each individual affected both in the short and in the long term and leads to economic impacts for the whole society, which must defray the costs of health treatment (Treyer et al., 2014).

The use of chemical compounds in the acid treatment (sulfuric acid), enzymatic hydrolysis (buffer) and fermentation (sodium phosphate) also contributes to impacts in the categories of human toxicity, metal depletion, marine, freshwater and terrestrial eco- toxicity, ionizing radiation and climate change.

There are no studies based on manure to ethanol that can be directly used as a benchmark for our findings. However, we find that these results are generally in line with those reported by other lignocellulosic bioethanol studies.

Moreover, in the climate change category there is an environ- mental impact ratio of 1.51 kg CO2 eq to 43.4 L of bioethanol pro- duced, corresponding to 0.0017 kg CO2 eq MJ-1, where 1 L of bioethanol corresponds to an energy content of 20.09 MJ (INEN, 2016). This value is less than the value obtained by Mayer et al. (2015), who determined 0.128 kg CO2 eq MJ-1 in a small-scale ethanol production from sugar cane where 54% of the CO2 eq emissions were at the industrial stage (0.06912 kg CO2 eq MJ-1). According to Rocha et al. (2014), at the industrial scale it is possible to achieve 0.00626 kg CO2 eq MJ or less, involving agricultural sugar cane production in the scope of the research.

According to Costa Junior et al. (2015) the total measured and estimated GHG emissions from housing to field application comprised of 15.0 + 7.4 kg CO2-eq animal which 80% corre- sponded to the field application of manure, this is approximately 12 kg CO2-eq animal-¹ For bioethanol production in the functional unit proposal in our study we identified a generate of 1.51 kg CO2- eq, approximately 25 feedlot cattle (0.06 kg CO2-eq animal-¹. The GHG estimated mitigation is 95% the same that Costa Junior et al. (2015) found for electricity cogeneration and N-fertilizer produc- tion from feedlot cattle manure.
Besides that, as presented previously in Table 1 from several assessment and production methods, the GWP from many ligno- cellulosics feedstooks is lower than from fossil fuel.

These analyses demonstrate that bioethanol derived from CM can be interesting for agro-industrial diversification. CM is a dangerous residue for the environment and its main impacts can be minimized when used in large-scale ethanol production.
34: Bioethanol production from CM, a residue that requires treat- ment for disposal, receives high impact compensation. There is an important environmental gain from ethanol production when it uses a raw material that is a residue from another process.

In the case of CM, the impact of these residues was reduced because the process eliminates the problem of adequate disposal of the manure, to which are assigned impacts on Global Warming Potential, Aquatic and Terrestrial Eutrophication potential. Ac- cording to Cherubini et al. (2015) the manure management system contributed as high as 59.7% and 78.6% of the total impact for freshwater eutrophication and terrestrial ecotoxicity, respectively. This arises because manure management can generate emissions such as carbon dioxide, ammonia, phosphorus and nitrates, which contribute to climate change, terrestrial acidification, freshwater eutrophication and marine eutrophication.

According to Battini et al. (2014), when manure was stored in an open tank and then used as fertilizer, up to 82% of the total impact was due to ammonia emissions. Similarly, this author analyzed the impacts on the employment of manure to produce biogas and determined that NOx emissions increase with the combustion of biogas and SO2 emissions decrease because of the fossil electricity substituted.
Thus, the use of manure for the bioethanol production may
reduce the impacts due to other common fates of this residue. The results of assessment of the impacts of the life cycle of bioethanol production from cattle manure are shown in Table 4.

Negative values of LCIA presented in the table are compensa- tions of the manure disposal and treatment impacts. Fig. 2 shows that the major environmental gains using CM as a bioethanol raw material occurred in the drying step, in which CM is an input. The impacts in several categories were negative. In the categories of marine eutrophication, terrestrial acidification, particulate matter formation, ozone depletion and climate change the impacts were 40%-100% negative and demonstrate the benefits of the use of CM as a raw material in the process. The negative values indicate sav- ings in the use of manure and the achievement of related envi- ronmental benefits, mainly the reduction of the release of compounds responsible for ozone depletion, human toxicity and water eutrophication. Ripa et al. (2017) also found that there was compensation in the AICV results. They interpreted that the pro- duction of organic compost from manure to substitute equivalent amounts of inorganic fertilizers saved impacts related to manure disposal, besides other gains from the use of natural products as more stabilized forms of carbon.

Cattle manure to bioethanol conversion avoids problems with pathogens present in animal manure that can cause a number of humans disease and environmental contamination by nutrients (eg, N and P) in the soil (Lorenz and Lal, 2016).

Despite the impacts that were avoided by the use of CM as the raw material, the drying was the step with the most environmental impact, due to the energy consumption and the emissions from CM during the drying.

In the method using harmonized category indicators at the midpoint level, all impact categories in the process were repre- sented. The most contributing processes to each relevant impact category were enzymatic hydrolysis and separation phases. In the enzymatic hydrolysis step, presented in Fig. 3, there were contri- butions in all categories due to energy, enzyme, disposal of the bio- waste in agriculture after hydrolysis, and use of buffer for pH control in the process. Gerbrandt et al. (2016) noted that the pro- portion of the enzyme used has a direct effect on greenhouse gas

(2015) the total measured and estimated GHG emissions from housing to field application comprised of 15.0 + 7.4 kg CO2-eq animal which 80% corre- sponded to the field application of manure, this is approximately 12 kg CO2-eq animal-¹ For bioethanol production in the functional unit proposal in our study we identified a generate of 1.51 kg CO2- eq, approximately 25 feedlot cattle (0.06 kg CO2-eq animal-¹. The GHG estimated mitigation is 95% the same that Costa Junior et al. (2015) found for electricity cogeneration and N-fertilizer produc- tion from feedlot cattle manure.

36: Bioethanol production from CM, a residue that requires treat- ment for disposal, receives high impact compensation. There is an important environmental gain from ethanol production when it uses a raw material that is a residue from another process.

In the case of CM, the impact of these residues was reduced because the process eliminates the problem of adequate disposal of the manure, to which are assigned impacts on Global Warming Potential, Aquatic and Terrestrial Eutrophication potential. Ac- cording to Cherubini et al. (2015) the manure management system contributed as high as 59.7% and 78.6% of the total impact for freshwater eutrophication and terrestrial ecotoxicity, respectively. This arises because manure management can generate emissions such as carbon dioxide, ammonia, phosphorus and nitrates, which contribute to climate change, terrestrial acidification, freshwater eutrophication and marine eutrophication.

According to Battini et al. (2014), when manure was stored in an open tank and then used as fertilizer, up to 82% of the total impact was due to ammonia emissions. Similarly, this author analyzed the impacts on the employment of manure to produce biogas and determined that NOx emissions increase with the combustion of biogas and SO2 emissions decrease because of the fossil electricity substituted.
Thus, the use of manure for the bioethanol production may
reduce the impacts due to other common fates of this residue. The results of assessment of the impacts of the life cycle of bioethanol production from cattle manure are shown in Table 4.

Negative values of LCIA presented in the table are compensa- tions of the manure disposal and treatment impacts. Fig. 2 shows that the major environmental gains using CM as a bioethanol raw material occurred in the drying step, in which CM is an input. The impacts in several categories were negative. In the categories of marine eutrophication, terrestrial acidification, particulate matter formation, ozone depletion and climate change the impacts were 40%-100% negative and demonstrate the benefits of the use of CM as a raw material in the process. The negative values indicate sav- ings in the use of manure and the achievement of related envi- ronmental benefits, mainly the reduction of the release of compounds responsible for ozone depletion, human toxicity and water eutrophication. Ripa et al. (2017) also found that there was compensation in the AICV results. They interpreted that the pro- duction of organic compost from manure to substitute equivalent amounts of inorganic fertilizers saved impacts related to manure disposal, besides other gains from the use of natural products as more stabilized forms of carbon.

Cattle manure to bioethanol conversion avoids problems with pathogens present in animal manure that can cause a number of humans disease and environmental contamination by nutrients (eg, N and P) in the soil (Lorenz and Lal, 2016).

Despite the impacts that were avoided by the use of CM as the raw material, the drying was the step with the most environmental impact, due to the energy consumption and the emissions from CM during the drying.

In the method using harmonized category indicators at the midpoint level, all impact categories in the process were repre- sented. The most contributing processes to each relevant impact category were enzymatic hydrolysis and separation phases. In the enzymatic hydrolysis step, presented in Fig. 3, there were contri- butions in all categories due to energy, enzyme, disposal of the bio- waste in agriculture after hydrolysis, and use of buffer for pH control in the process. Gerbrandt et al. (2016) noted that the pro- portion of the enzyme used has a direct effect on greenhouse gas

emissions due to the carbon intensity of the enzyme production. Electricity was not used in the separation of the solid phase after the pretreatment because filtration by gravity was used. However, in this step of the process improvement will be possible by the use of centrifugation. Electricity added impacts related mainly to climate change, human toxicity, freshwater and marine ecotoxicity. Climate change and human toxicity are the main contributors to the total of the potential human health impacts (HHI). The HHI corrupts the quality of life of each individual affected both in the short and in the long term and leads to economic impacts for the whole society, which must defray the costs of health treatment (Treyer et al., 2014).

The use of chemical compounds in the acid treatment (sulfuric acid), enzymatic hydrolysis (buffer) and fermentation (sodium phosphate) also contributes to impacts in the categories of human toxicity, metal depletion, marine, freshwater and terrestrial eco- toxicity, ionizing radiation and climate change.

There are no studies based on manure to ethanol that can be directly used as a benchmark for our findings. However, we find that these results are generally in line with those reported by other lignocellulosic bioethanol studies.

Moreover, in the climate change category there is an environ- mental impact ratio of 1.51 kg CO2 eq to 43.4 L of bioethanol pro- duced, corresponding to 0.0017 kg CO2 eq MJ-1, where 1 L of bioethanol corresponds to an energy content of 20.09 MJ (INEN, 2016). This value is less than the value obtained by Mayer et al. According to Costa Junior et al. (2015) the total measured and estimated GHG emissions from housing to field application comprised of 15.0 + 7.4 kg CO2-eq animal which 80% corre- sponded to the field application of manure, this is approximately 12 kg CO2-eq animal-¹ For bioethanol production in the functional unit proposal in our study we identified a generate of 1.51 kg CO2- eq, approximately 25 feedlot cattle (0.06 kg CO2-eq animal-¹. The GHG estimated mitigation is 95% the same that Costa Junior et al. (2015) found for electricity cogeneration and N-fertilizer produc- tion from feedlot cattle manure.
Besides that, as presented previously in Table 1 from several assessment and production methods, the GWP from many ligno- cellulosics feedstooks is lower than from fossil fuel.

These analyses demonstrate that bioethanol derived from CM can be interesting for agro-industrial diversification. CM is a dangerous residue for the environment and its main impacts can be minimized when used in large-scale ethanol production.
39: Bioethanol production from CM, a residue that requires treat- ment for disposal, receives high impact compensation. There is an important environmental gain from ethanol production when it uses a raw material that is a residue from another process.

In the case of CM, the impact of these residues was reduced because the process eliminates the problem of adequate disposal of the manure, to which are assigned impacts on Global Warming Potential, Aquatic and Terrestrial Eutrophication potential. Ac- cording to Cherubini et al. (2015) the manure management system contributed as high as 59.7% and 78.6% of the total impact for freshwater eutrophication and terrestrial ecotoxicity, respectively. This arises because manure management can generate emissions such as carbon dioxide, ammonia, phosphorus and nitrates, which contribute to climate change, terrestrial acidification, freshwater eutrophication and marine eutrophication.

According to Battini et al. (2014), when manure was stored in an open tank and then used as fertilizer, up to 82% of the total impact was due to ammonia emissions. Similarly, this author analyzed the impacts on the employment of manure to produce biogas and determined that NOx emissions increase with the combustion of biogas and SO2 emissions decrease because of the fossil electricity substituted.
Thus, the use of manure for the bioethanol production may
reduce the impacts due to other common fates of this residue. The results of assessment of the impacts of the life cycle of bioethanol production from cattle manure are shown in Table 4.

Negative values of LCIA presented in the table are compensa- tions of the manure disposal and treatment impacts. Fig. 2 shows that the major environmental gains using CM as a bioethanol raw material occurred in the drying step, in which CM is an input. The impacts in several categories were negative. In the categories of marine eutrophication, terrestrial acidification, particulate matter formation, ozone depletion and climate change the impacts were 40%-100% negative and demonstrate the benefits of the use of CM as a raw material in the process. The negative values indicate sav- ings in the use of manure and the achievement of related envi- ronmental benefits, mainly the reduction of the release of compounds responsible for ozone depletion, human toxicity and water eutrophication. Ripa et al. (2017) also found that there was compensation in the AICV results. They interpreted that the pro- duction of organic compost from manure to substitute equivalent amounts of inorganic fertilizers saved impacts related to manure disposal, besides other gains from the use of natural products as more stabilized forms of carbon.

Cattle manure to bioethanol conversion avoids problems with pathogens present in animal manure that can cause a number of humans disease and environmental contamination by nutrients (eg, N and P) in the soil (Lorenz and Lal, 2016).

Despite the impacts that were avoided by the use of CM as the raw material, the drying was the step with the most environmental impact, due to the energy consumption and the emissions from CM during the drying.

In the method using harmonized category indicators at the midpoint level, all impact categories in the process were repre- sented. The most contributing processes to each relevant impact category were enzymatic hydrolysis and separation phases. In the enzymatic hydrolysis step, presented in Fig. 3, there were contri- butions in all categories due to energy, enzyme, disposal of the bio- waste in agriculture after hydrolysis, and use of buffer for pH control in the process. Gerbrandt et al. (2016) noted that the pro- portion of the enzyme used has a direct effect on greenhouse gas
Fig. 2. The characterization (%) of the life cycle impact assessment from CM bioethanol production by ReCiPe Midpoint (H) V1.06.

emissions due to the carbon intensity of the enzyme production. Electricity was not used in the separation of the solid phase after the pretreatment because filtration by gravity was used. However, in this step of the process improvement will be possible by the use of centrifugation. Electricity added impacts related mainly to climate change, human toxicity, freshwater and marine ecotoxicity. Climate change and human toxicity are the main contributors to the total of the potential human health impacts (HHI). The HHI corrupts the quality of life of each individual affected both in the short and in the long term and leads to economic impacts for the whole society, which must defray the costs of health treatment (Treyer et al., 2014).

The use of chemical compounds in the acid treatment (sulfuric acid), enzymatic hydrolysis (buffer) and fermentation (sodium phosphate) also contributes to impacts in the categories of human toxicity, metal depletion, marine, freshwater and terrestrial eco- toxicity, ionizing radiation and climate change.

40: From the endpoint assessment presented in Fig. 4 it appears that the drying and enzymatic hydrolysis steps were the main con- tributors to damages. Bioethanol production from CM has low environmental impacts on most of the categories in long term. They are discriminated in categories that are commonly grouped into

resource and human health damages. It was observed some result in climate change (CC), human toxicity (HT), particulate matter formation (PMF), and fossil resource depletion (FD). Ecosystems damages from the enzymatic hydrolysis step were minimal. The damage total values for CM bioethanol production by the meth- odology studied in this paper were -3.55E-06 DALY, 4.93-09 species.yr and $16.94 for human health, ecosystems and natural resources, respectively.
The inputs that led to these impacts were energy consumption and the use of H2SO4 and NaOH in addition to other chemicals involved in cleaning and waste treatment.

By evaluating the categories of impact by the single score characterization (mPt), it was possible to identify that the category of fossil depletion is the one with the largest contribution to energy consumption. These results were observed mainly in the drying, acid pre-treatment, fermentation and distillation steps.

Each step can be more eco-efficient if the energy, inputs and wastes involved in the process are controlled (Roy et al., 2009). The reduction of energy consumption guarantees environmental gains over the long term, but it depends on the way in which energy is generated to guarantee more gains. The impacts affected by the quantities of raw materials and waste depend directly on the effi- ciency of the process.

The statistical evaluation was performed using Monte Carlo Simulation (MCS) with SimaPro® V. 7.3.2 using the normalized data. Monte Carlo analysis represents a formal mechanism for appro- priately predicting uncertain inputs, which is a relevant analytical approach to evaluate CM bioethanol production.

Assessing uncertainties, the modeling endpoint is overloaded with large uncertainties due to the many unknowns in the system and the long time horizon of some of the effects involved (Cavalett et al., 2013).

Based on the probability distribution of the computed category indicators, the uncertainty ranges for the characterized LCIA pro- files of CM bioethanol production were derived. The 95% confidence intervals given in Table 5 indicated that in 95% of the cases the LCIA results for CM bioethanol production life cycle would fall within the range (U, V) (Guo and Murphy, 2012). To normalize the data, the software considered the standardization factors that are deter- mined by each relative contribution in a given category. The coef- ficient of variation (CV) is the indicator of dispersion in the category

indicator data. The CVs suggest that a large degree of uncertainty is contained in the results of some categories, especially ionizing ra- diation, freshwater ecotoxicity, and freshwater eutrophication.

Small variations in absolute values show more reliability of re- sults and indicate that category expresses more impacts. Thus, the average values for the main categories impacted by the process can be considered reliable.

After the LCIA has been applied as a tool for analysis and design of processes (Gillani et al., 2010), the results are relevant to bio- ethanol production from CM. The damage category uncertainties were smaller than the ranges observed in the individual categories. The mean data of damage categories with error bars are shown in Fig. 5.

Improvements can still be inserted into the process, such as the use of wood-fired to generate heat in substitution of electricity and minimization of water content. However, exploitation of hydrolysis and distillation wastewater for crop fertilization and/or biogas production is a good choice. Results obtained with sugar cane have already shown that there is an increase of the eco-efficiency when the residue (vinasse) is used in soil fertilization (Silalertruksa et al., 2015).

Next, in the sensitivity analysis, the energy content was changed from electricity to wood-fired to generate heat (scenario 2) in order to evaluate if it is a viable alternative to reduce the environmental impacts in the process. In addition, a modification was proposed in the separation phases with acid neutralization/solid washing (scenario 3), replacing the great water content used in solid washing by NaOH solution (50%|48 kg). The results of these sce- narios, compared to the original scenario (scenario 1) are presented in Table 6, with the reduction of impact percentages obtained from improvements of each scenario. The process is less sensitive to the significant reduction of wash water used. According to Budsberg et al. (2015) the NaOH production also use water and the impacts could not be negligible.
Large differences in observed impacts were due to the electricity substitution by heat from wood-fired, mainly in relation to the damage of natural resources.
Initial scenario (S1) compared with S4 that combines the im- provements we obtain Fig. 6. The impacts and the uncertainties decrease with the proposed changes.

The statistical evaluation was performed using Monte Carlo Simulation (MCS) with SimaPro® V. 7.3.2 using the normalized data. Monte Carlo analysis represents a formal mechanism for appro- priately predicting uncertain inputs, which is a relevant analytical approach to evaluate CM bioethanol production.

Assessing uncertainties, the modeling endpoint is overloaded with large uncertainties due to the many unknowns in the system and the long time horizon of some of the effects involved (Cavalett et al., 2013).

47: Thus, the use of CM for the production of bioethanol is an alternative for the future. It is understood that the different ligno- cellulosic materials should be used as raw material integrated with the other options that the region offers. Thus, regions that have livestock production can provide manure and other crops that are suitable for bioethanol production according to the agricultural and environmental vocation. This modifications can improve the lignocellulosic ethanol production and minimize the environ- mental impacts.

The diversification of raw materials can be done using a classical method of production of lignocellulosic ethanol with acid pre- treatment, enzymatic hydrolysis, fermentation and distillation, as it was used in this work, modifying the preliminary stages of drying and milling depending on the raw material, as well as optimizing reactant concentrations and the times of each step.
48: This study presents the results from a pilot survey performed using experimental data collected with the proposed equipment to determine the LCA of bioethanol production from cattle manure in a functional unit of 1000 kg. From the LCIA results, it can be concluded from midpoint indicators with normalized data that the principal impacts were on human toxicity, freshwater eutrophica- tion, terrestrial and marine ecotoxicity and fossil depletion. The step that contributes most to environmental impacts is drying.

The use of cattle manure for bioethanol production eliminates the need for disposal treatment and the use of residual raw mate- rial, thus counterbalancing the environmental impacts of bio- ethanol production.

The inputs/outputs that have the greatest environmental im- pacts are energy consumption, drying emissions, sulfurio acid in the pretreatment, buffer in enzymatic hydrolysis and sodium phos- phate in fermentation.

Though this system provides economic benefits to farmers through a biomass source that would have been otherwise lost, the study gives clear evidence that the life cycle of this bioethanol production system is significantly influenced by the inputs in the production. Our results from LCIA show that the bioethanol pro- duction from CM has low impacts on most of the categories. By endpoint indicators was observed impacts only in climate change (CC), human toxicity (HT), particulate matter formation (PMF), and fossil resource depletion (FD).
Uncertainties for characterized LCIA profiles of CM bioethanol production.

53: This study presents the results from a pilot survey performed using experimental data collected with the proposed equipment to determine the LCA of bioethanol production from cattle manure in a functional unit of 1000 kg. From the LCIA results, it can be concluded from midpoint indicators with normalized data that the principal impacts were on human toxicity, freshwater eutrophica- tion, terrestrial and marine ecotoxicity and fossil depletion. The step that contributes most to environmental impacts is drying.

The use of cattle manure for bioethanol production eliminates the need for disposal treatment and the use of residual raw mate- rial, thus counterbalancing the environmental impacts of bio- ethanol production.

The inputs/outputs that have the greatest environmental im- pacts are energy consumption, drying emissions, sulfurio acid in the pretreatment, buffer in enzymatic hydrolysis and sodium phos- phate in fermentation.

Though this system provides economic benefits to farmers through a biomass source that would have been otherwise lost, the study gives clear evidence that the life cycle of this bioethanol production system is significantly influenced by the inputs in the production. Our results from LCIA show that the bioethanol pro- duction from CM has low impacts on most of the categories. By endpoint indicators was observed impacts only in climate change (CC), human toxicity (HT), particulate matter formation (PMF), and fossil resource depletion (FD).
Uncertainties for characterized LCIA profiles of CM bioethanol production.
